coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent determination of graphene quantum dots in water samples
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Benítez-Martínez, Sandra
Valcárcel, Miguel
coredata.dc:description: This work presents a simple, fast and sensitive method for the preconcentration and quantification of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in aqueous samples. GQDs are considered an object of analysis (analyte) not an analytical tool which is the most frequent situation in Analytical Nanoscience and Nanotechnology. This approach is based on the preconcentration of graphene quantum dots on an anion exchange sorbent by solid phase extraction and their subsequent elution prior fluorimetric analysis of the solution containing graphene quantum dots. Parameters of the extraction procedure such as sample volume, type of solvent, sample pH, sample flow rate and elution conditions were investigated in order to achieve extraction efficiency. The limits of detection and quantification were 7.5 μg L−1 and 25 μg L−1, respectively. The precision for 200 μg L−1, expressed as %RSD, was 2.8%. Recoveries percentages between 86.9 and 103.9% were obtained for two different concentration levels. Interferences from other nanoparticles were studied and no significant changes were observed at the concentration levels tested. Consequently, the optimized procedure has great potential to be applied to the determination of graphene quantum dots at trace levels in drinking and environmental waters.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Solid phase extraction
Fluorescence
Water samples
Interferences

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Functionalization of single-walled carbon nanotubes and graphene
Application of the determination of GQDs in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HR-TEM images of GQDs and (b) FT-MIR spectra of GQDs showing the presence of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups.
General scheme proposed for the retention and elution mechanisms.
Study of the sample volume passed through the solid phase extraction column.

Study of the pH for the retention of GQDs by SAX (strong anion exchange) sorbent. The GQDs concentration used was 50 mg L−1. a) Fluorescence intensities of the GQDs solutions at different pH. b) Fluorescence intensities of the GQDs solutions at different pH after their pass through the SAX column.

Study of the elution volume. A) Percentage of fluorescence intensity of the GQDs solution before its pass through the SAX (strong anion exchange) cartridge. B) Percentage of fluorescence intensity of GQDs after elution with pH 13 adjusted ultrapure water.
Fluorescence intensities of the recovered GQDs. The inset depicts a calibration graph of GQDs standards aqueous solutions under the optimal conditions.
Recovery study of spiked waters samples.

row.entry: Found concentration of GQDs a (μg L−1)

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Average of seven independent spiked samples±CI (confidence interval, p < 0.05).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Interference study of spiked water samples expressed as % of recovery.

row.entry: Found concentration of GQDs a (μg L−1)

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Average of five independent spiked samples±CI (confidence interval, p < 0.05).

head.ce:title: Fluorescent determination of graphene quantum dots in water samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, University of Córdoba, E-14071 Córdoba, Spain

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work presents a simple, fast and sensitive method for the preconcentration and quantification of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in aqueous samples. GQDs are considered an object of analysis (analyte) not an analytical tool which is the most frequent situation in Analytical Nanoscience and Nanotechnology. This approach is based on the preconcentration of graphene quantum dots on an anion exchange sorbent by solid phase extraction and their subsequent elution prior fluorimetric analysis of the solution containing graphene quantum dots. Parameters of the extraction procedure such as sample volume, type of solvent, sample pH, sample flow rate and elution conditions were investigated in order to achieve extraction efficiency. The limits of detection and quantification were 7.5 μg L−1 and 25 μg L−1, respectively. The precision for 200 μg L−1, expressed as %RSD, was 2.8%. Recoveries percentages between 86.9 and 103.9% were obtained for two different concentration levels. Interferences from other nanoparticles were studied and no significant changes were observed at the concentration levels tested. Consequently, the optimized procedure has great potential to be applied to the determination of graphene quantum dots at trace levels in drinking and environmental waters.

ce:section.ce:para: In the last years, the advances in nanotechnology and the application of a widely variety of engineered nanoparticles in the industry and commercial products make human and environmental exposure increase every day [1]. Graphene, a two dimensional one-atom thick with sp2 hybridization carbon nanoparticle, and graphene family nanomaterials have attracted much attention since the graphene first isolation in 2004 due to their exceptional electronic, mechanical, optical and thermal properties [2,3]. Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), an emerging graphene based luminescent nanoparticles have become in object of interest in the scientific community due to their electronic and optical properties. GQDs are graphene nanosheets smaller than 100 nm lateral size and diameters between 3 and 20 nm. These nanoparticles can be as single-, double- or multi-layer [4]. Quantum confinement and edge effect make them exhibit interesting properties such as fluorescent activity, robust chemical inertness, excellent photostability, high biocompatibility and low toxicity [5]. These properties confer it a variety of potential uses in several scopes, mainly, sensors [6] and biosensors [7], bioimaging [8], drug delivery [9] and photovoltaic devices [10]. In spite of the fact that GQDs are defined as low toxic and high biocompatible nanoparticles, there are some works that put into question these features. In vivo studies in mice revealed that the GQDs were mainly accumulated in liver, spleen, lung, kidney and tumor sites [11]. Otherwise, the theory of lateral dimension explains that the in vivo biodistribution and manifestation of toxicity would be lower as smaller nanomaterial size, so GQDs smaller than 5 nm would be eliminated with urine [12]. Due to the GQDs biosafety issue still been unclear and the increasing production and use of graphene nanomaterials could increase the risk of environmental widespread, methodologies that allow the determination of these nanoparticles should be developed. Nevertheless, at present, there are only a few analytical approaches for this purpose. Some methodologies have been described for carbon nanotubes [13], gold nanoparticles [14,15] and, most recently, graphene oxide [16].

In this work, a simple, rapid and sensitive approach for the determination of GQDs in natural and tap water samples was developed. The interaction of GQDs with the charged sorbent through ionic interactions and their subsequent elution lead to the preconcentration of GQDs. The fluorescence emission of the eluted graphene nanosheets has been used as analytical signal for the quantification of the presence of GQDs. As far as we are concerned, this is the first approach to the determination of graphene quantum dots from environmental and drinking waters.

All chemical reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Citric acid (≥99.0%), used as precursor in the GQDs synthesis procedure, ammonium acetate (≥98.0%), nitric (≥69.0%) and sulfuric (≥95%) acids, HPLC-grade acetone (≥99.8%) acetonitrile (≥99.8%), ethyl acetate, sodium sulfate anhydrous (≥99.0%), sodium phosphate dehydrate (98.5–101.0%) and humic acid were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich (Madrid, Spain). Sodium hydroxide, hydrochloric acid, N,N-dimethylformamide (≥99.8%), dimethyl sulfoxide (≥99.5%) and hydrogen peroxide (33%) were obtained from Panreac Chemical, SAU (Barcelona, Spain). HPLC-grade methanol (≥99.9%) was purchased from Carlo Erba Reagents (Barcelona, Spain). Single Wall Carbon Nanotubes (SWCNT) were obtained from ShenzhenNanotechPortCo.Ltd (NTP) (China), with a purity over 90%, an outer diameter of <2 nm, a length of 5–15 mm and a surface area of 500–700 m2 g−1. Graphene (avangraphene-2, <6 sheets) was supplied by Avanzare Innovacion Tecnologica SL (Logroño, Spain). Commercial gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), 10 nm sized, were purchased also from Sigma–Aldrich. Ultrapure water was obtained from a Milli-Q purification system (Millipore, Madrid, Spain) with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ/cm and used for preparation of all solutions.

Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded on a PTI QuantaMaster™ spectrofluorimeter from Photon Technology International (Barcelona, Spain) equipped with a 75 W xenon short arc lamp and an 814 PTM detection system. The software FeliX32 was used for data acquisition and instrument control. The excitation and emission slits were both 6 nm wide. All measurements were made at room temperature, using micro quartz cuvettes of 10 mm lightpath.
Fourier transform mid infrared (FT-MIR) spectra were obtained on a Bruker Tensor 27 FT-MIR spectrophotometer equipped with a Hyperion 2000 microscope, using KBr pellets.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were obtained on a JEOL JEM 2010 electron microscope available at the Research Support Service (SCAI) of the University of Córdoba. The instrument has a point-to-point resolution of 0.194 nm and was operated at a medium acceleration voltage of 200 kV.

GQDs were obtained by pyrolysis of citric acid, similarly to that described in Refs. [6,16] using a slightly modify procedure similar to that described by Dong et al. [17]. To this end, an amount of 2 g of citric acid was placed in a 10 mL vial and heated at 200 °C on a thermoblock (JP Selecta, Barcelona, Spain) for 30 min. During this time the citric acid changed from a white dust to a dark orange viscous liquid. The resulting liquid was added dropwise to 100 mL of a 0.25 M NaOH solution under vigorous stirring. The GQD aqueous solution obtained was kept stirring for 10 min, adjusted to pH 7 with nitric acid and stored at 4 °C in an amber bottle.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functionalization of single-walled carbon nanotubes and graphene
ce:section.ce:para: Carboxylated single-walled carbon nanotubes (c-SWCNTs), graphene oxide (GO) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) have been considered as potential interferences in this work. AuNPs were of commercial origin. C-SWCNTs were prepared by adding 100 mg of SWCNT to 20 mL of a 3:1 H2SO4/HNO3 mixture into a glass flask similar to the procedure described by Lopez-Lorente et al. [13]. The mixture was refluxed for 1 h. After that, diluted fractions were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min and washed with ultrapure water. The centrifugation and washing processes were repeated until the supernatant phase stopped having acidic pH. Finally, carboxylated derivates were dried at 60 °C in a heater. The same procedure was performed in order to obtain graphene oxide (GO), being diluted fractions of treated graphene centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min due to the less size of these nanoparticles. After acid treatment, SWCNTs and graphene posses carboxylic (–COOH) groups on the sidewalls and open ends (in the case of SWCNTs) being negatively charged, which make them highly water soluble.

Commercial SAX (Strong Anion Exchange) column with a capacity of 1 mL and 50 mg of sorbent (Biotage, Hengoed, United Kingdom) was selected for the SPE extraction of GQDs. SAX is a quaternary amine (functionalized with a chloride counter ion) silica sorbent with an average particle size of 52 μm, a pore diameter of 56 Å and a surface area of 480 m2 g−1, packed in a polypropylene column (volume: 1 mL; inner diameter: 6 mm) and fitted with polyethylene frits. This SAX column was rinsed with 1 mL of ultrapure water, followed by 1 mL of 0.1 M and 15 mM ammonium acetate, respectively. Then, 20 mL of an aqueous solution containing GQDs at pH 7.0 was passed through the column at a constant flow rate of 1 mL min−1. A daily prepared 0.5 mg/mL GQDs aqueous solution standard was adjusted to pH 7.0 with diluted HNO3. After loading the sample, the sorbent was dried by passing air through the column during 3 min. The nanoparticles retained were finally eluted with 0.5 mL of 0.25 M of NaOH at a flow rate of 0.1 mL min−1. For the experimental performance, SAX column was connected to an Accu-rated™ two collars PVC pump tubing (with an inner diameter of 1.14 mm) which was coupled to the peristaltic pump in order to suck out all solutions through the SAX sorbent. The obtained extract was measured directly in a spectrofluorimeter.

Fluorescence measurements were performed at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm recording the emission between 400 and 600 nm using a PTI QuantaMaster™ spectrofluorimeter from Photon Technology International (Barcelona, Spain) equipped with a 75 W xenon short arc lamp and an 814 PTM detection system. The software FeliX32 was used for data acquisition and instrument control. The excitation and emission slits were both 6 nm wide. All measurements were made at room temperature, using a 10 mm lightpath micro quartz cuvette. The extract from the SPE procedure was transferred to the micro cuvette and kept 5 min out of the sample compartment in order to the stabilization of GQDs in the alkaline conditions (pH 13.0) of the elution solvent. This time was required to obtain a stable fluorescence signal because GQDs pass from pH 7.0 in the sample to pH 13.0 after elution, and it is well know that fluorescence of these nanoparticles change with the pH. After 5 min the fluorescent signal was very stable. Then, the fluorescence spectra were acquired.

The drinking water was collected directly from a tap in the lab and the natural spring water samples were collected manually in the surrounding area of Montilla, Córdoba, Spain. All samples were collected in amber glass bottles, which were rinsed 3 times-fold with the sample, filled out of volume and stored at 4 °C. Real samples were filtered through a 0.2 μm nylon filter prior SPE procedure.

GQDs used in this work were surface-pasivated circular nanosheets with an average diameter of 4.1 ± 0.7 nm, which were characterized by HR-TEM. FT-MIR revealed the presence of namely carboxyl and hydroxyl functional groups which confers them good water stability and solubility. The emission of the GQDs obtained by this route was excitation wavelength independent, excitation at different wavelength do not causes any shift in the maximum fluorescence emission. They exhibited highest fluorescence emission at 474 nm when they were excited at 379 nm. Fig. 1 shows (a) HR-TEM images and (b) FT-MIR spectrum of GQDs after synthesis.

The GQDs concentration was 4.5 mg mL−1 and the GQDs quantum yield was 4.5% using quinine sulfate as reference. Functional groups, presents only at their edges, allow the interaction between GQDs and SPE sorbent. Fig. 2 shows a general scheme proposed the mechanisms for the retention and the elution of GQDs in aqueous solutions.

The quantification of GQDs was possible thanks to the strong interaction between the quaternary amine bonded sorbent and the fluorescent carbon nanoparticles. Carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, present at the edges of GQDs, interacted with the positive charge of the sorbent through ionic interactions. The quaternary amine kept permanent positive charged across the pH range. Elution by organic solvents did not release the nanoparticles; nevertheless, ultrapure water adjusted to pH 13 accomplished the elution of GQDs from the sorbent by eliminating the charge of the functional groups.
The studies for the optimization of the variables presented below were made in triplicate.

The optimal sample loading volume was investigated by passing different volumes of GQDs aqueous solution through the SAX column. The volumes investigated ranged from 1 to 25 mL. All samples were spiked with GQDs adjusted to pH 7 at a concentration level of 10 mg L−1. Each eluted sample volume (1, 5, 10, 15, 20 and 25 mL) was directly analyzed milliliter by milliliter in the spertrofluorimeter. As can be seen in Fig. 3 , the signal of 1 mL of GQDs sample after it pass through SAX column was the same as the signal of the ultrapure water and no fluorescence emission was detected after passing 25 mL. So, the loading capacity of the sorbent was high than 0.005 mg GQDs/mg of SAX calculated as the ratio between the amount of GQDs in 25 mL of sample and the amount of sorbent used for the extraction, 50 mg. Under the studied conditions the breakthrough volume was not achieved since the GQDs retention efficiency was observed for increasing sample volumes. As a compromise to achieve good reproducibility, high sensitivity and short analysis times, 20 mL were chosen for all subsequent extractions.

In a traditional SPE procedure, sample pH plays an important role since it determines the ionic or molecular form of an analyte and therefore, its interaction with the sorbent. The pKa of a molecular functional group is defined as the pH at which 50% of this group in solution is charged, and 50% are uncharged. Each pH unit change affects the percentage of charged or uncharged groups by a factor of 10, so it is sensible to perform extractions at a pH at least 2 pH units from the pKa value, to ensure that 99.5% of the functional groups are in the desired state of ionization.

In this paper we use GQDs nanoparticles as analyte and we explore a wide range of pH in order to study their behavior with the sorbent. For this study, 50 mg L−1 GQDs aqueous solution was adjusted a different pH (3, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11 and 13) and 1 mL of them was passed through the cartridge at a low flow rate to ensure the retention of the nanoparticles. Fluorescence of the solution was measured before and after passing through the column. As can be seen in Fig. 4 (a), at low concentrations of GQDs (50 mg L−1) FL (fluorescence) emission peaked at pH 13, being slightly lower at pH 11 and 9, decreasing at pH 8 and 10 and reducing markedly their emission below neutral pH.

This behavior could be explained due to an aggregation phenomena when GQDs nanoparticles, which exist at low concentration, are pH adjusted. When the concentration of this kind of nanoparticles is high the FL emission follows a different trend [6,16] peaking at pH 7, decreasing markedly with increasing pH in alkaline media and more gradually in acidic media. Therefore, it can be concluded that GQDs synthesized by bottom-up method using citric acid as carbon precursor are pH-dependent at low and at high concentrations. When GQDs, at low concentration and different pH values, passed through the cartridge (Fig. 4(b)) it was observed that FL emission was almost equal at pH 13, only 3% of the signal decreased, so the interaction with the sorbent did not take place and the retention of the GQDs was not achieved. When the sample was passed at pH 6, fluorescence decreased a 78%. At pH 3 and 5 the signal of the solution passed through the column was reduced a 90% approximately. In this case, the retention of GQDs was observed into the cartridge polyethylene frit. It was attributed to an aggregation phenomenon caused by a partial protonation of the functional groups at the edge of the nanoparticles. At pH 8, 9 and 11 fluorescence decreased between 96 and 98%. The highest reduction of the FL emission was reached when sample was passed at pH 7 and 10 with a decrease >99%. Neutral pH was selected for the procedure development taking into account that natural waters usually have a pH between 6.5 and 8.5.

The sample was passed through the SAX column by using a peristaltic pump. With the purpose of optimizing the flow rate, 20 mL of sample were passed through a conditioned SPE cartridge at different rates: 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1, 1.25, 1.5 and 2 mL min−1. Results showed that no significant differences of the analytical signal were achieved at all studied flow rates. For this reason, and taking into account the manufacturer's specifications, 1 mL min−1 was selected as optimal sample flow rate.

Six different organic solvents, namely, acetonitrile, ethyl acetate, N,N-Dimethylformamide, dimethyl sulfoxide, methanol and acetone as well as pH 13.00 adjusted ultrapure water were tested in order to achieve the GQDs elution. Methanol and acetone were also tested at pH 3, 5, 7, 10 and 12. In all cases, 1 mL of solvent was passed through the loaded column at a flow rate of 0.1 mL min−1. As a result, no one of the organic solvents tested was able to elute the GQDs from the SAX matrix, even those which the pH was adjusted. The best results for the elution of the nanoparticles from the sorbent were achieved using pH 13 adjusted ultrapure water. Further experiments were performed in order to optimize the eluent volume. In order to obtain a high preconcentration factor with the highest recovery, 0.3, 0.4 and 0.5 mL of solvent were tested in the same flow rate (0.1 mL min−1). Fig. 5 shows that 0.5 mL were enough for the elution of 97% of the GQDs at least. Considering 0.5 mL as optimal elution volume, the theoretical concentration factor would be 40× (taking into account that the initial sample volume is 20 mL).

The analytical performance of the proposed method was studied in order to evaluate its usefulness for quantitative analyses. The linearity of the method was then studied using spiked deionized water standards. The GQDS added concentrations ranged from 25 to 500 μg L−1. The spiked samples were subjected to the above described optimized extraction method. A linear response was obtained in the range of 25–500 μg L−1 by plotting the FL emission of GQDs (at 474 nm) in a 0.25 M NaOH aqueous solution versus the spiked analyte concentration (I = (545723 ± 7037)[GQDs]+(35325 ± 1358), R2 = 0.9992). Each concentration level was analyzed in triplicate. Error bars depicts the standard deviation of the mean value. Fig. 6 shows the fluorescence intensity of some GQDs concentrations after the retention-elution procedure. The inset of the figure shows the calibration curve of the proposed method.

The theoretical limits of detection (LOD) and quantification (LOQ) were calculated as 3Sa/b and 10Sa/b, respectively, Sa being the standard error of the intercept and b the slope of de calibration curve (n = 3). The values found were a LOD of 7.5 μg L−1 and 25 μg L−1 as LOQ. The precision, expressed as relative standard deviation (RSD) in terms of repeatability (n = 5), was 2.85%.at a concentration level of 200 μg L−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the determination of GQDs in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of the proposed method was evaluated by the application of the optimized procedure to two real water samples, namely, tap and natural spring water. Despite the fact that no GQDs was expected to be found on them, real samples were spiked with these nanoparticles at five concentration levels, 75, 125, 200, 250 and 375 μg L−1 for each one, before performing the recovery studies. In order to avoid interferences from particulate matter, the spiked real samples were filtered through a 0.2 μm nylon filter prior to their pass through the SAX column. GQDs passed unhindered through the filter while particles in water were retained. After sample loading and prior to the elution step, 1 mL of 15 mM ammonium acetate 20% methanol was passed in order to eliminate interferences from the sample. Relative recovery values were calculated as the percentage of the ratio between the concentration found in the spiked samples after performing the extraction procedure and the initially spiked analyte concentration. Table 1 summarizes the GQDs recoveries obtained for the two real water samples ranging from 87.1 to 101.0% and 91.6–103.9% for natural spring and tap water, respectively, with RSDs not higher than 6.9%. The RSD within analysis ranges from 0.2 to 0.8% (n = 7) in both waters.

A study of interferences from other nanoparticles that could be presents in natural waters were performed by adding, separately, c-SWCNT (from 5 to 15 μm length), GO (from 0.1 to 2 μm wide) and AuNPs (10 nm sized) to the GQDs spiked real water samples. The influence of the organic matter has been studied using humic acids as standard. The influence of molecular ions has been evaluated by the addition of NaSO4 and Na2HPO4 to the real water sample. For this study, the same procedure as described above for the extraction of GQDs from tap and natural spring water – including a filtration step of the sample prior to their pass through the column and an interference remover step prior to elution – was performed. Real water samples were spiked with 375 μg L−1 of GQDs and two different concentrations of c-SWCNT, GO, AuNPs, humic acid, sodium sulfate and sodium phosphate (25 and 50 mg L−1) were added to the real sample. Prior to their pass through the cartridge, prepared real samples were filter by a 0.2 μm nylon syringe filter. As a result c-SWCNT, GO and AuNPs were retained in the filters. Interferences were studied for natural spring water samples. The recoveries obtained are summarized in Table 2 . As can be seen, good recoveries were obtained within the range of 95.9–101.9% for c-SWCNT, 96.2–104.3% for GO, 90.44–101.1% for AuNPs, 94.9–102.9% for NaSO4 and 92.1–101.9% for Na2HPO4. In the case of humic acids recoveries ranged from 122.7 to 136.68% and from 137 to 150.6% for 25 and 50 mg L−1, respectively. Thus indicates that despite part of the humic acids were retained in the filter (because it change from white to light brown) other part of them pass through the nylon syringe filter and both GQDs and humic acids were retained in the SAX sorbent. Both of them also were eluted and humic acids affected the fluorescence emission of the GQDs. They probably interact through electrostatic interactions and π–π staking. The charge transfer could occur from the humic acid to the GQDs, which acted as acceptor, and for this reason the fluorescence intensity increased resulting in high recoveries. If 1 mL of 6% v/v H2O2 and 1 mL of ultrapure water are passed after the sample loading recoveries can be improved, 96.8–105.3% and 97.7–106.76% for 25 and 50 mg L−1, respectively. The use of hydrogen peroxide contributes to eliminate the organic matter effect. Each sample was analyzed in quintuplicate in order to evaluate the precision of the method. The coefficients of variation ranged from 3.4% to 4.9%. Table 2 summarized the recoveries after the addition of the interferences.
The results suggest that the proposed method can be used for the extraction, preconcentration and quantification of GQDs in tap and natural spring waters.

This work presents a novel and very simple and sensitive method for the determination of GQDs in water samples. GQDs are retained in a strong anion exchange column for their preconcentration being recovered in an aqueous solution and measured in a spectrofluorimeter using the fluorescence of the GQDs as analytical signal for the quantification. A previous clean-up step (filtration through 0.2 μm syringe filter) was incorporated in order to avoid interferences from the particulated matter and from other nanoparticles. The method presents several advantages: i) the preconcentration step is automatic; ii) the time of analysis is very short; iii) the used solvent volume is reduced; iv) no organic solvent is needed for the elution step, just pH adjusted ultrapure water is used; v) good recovery values are provided.

In addition, this work supposes a new approach in the detection and quantification of nanoparticles in the environment. The consideration of nanoparticles as an object of analysis is the less developed facet of the traditional Analytical Nanoscience and Nanotechnology.
The authors have declared no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to thank to the Junta de Andalucía (Project FQM 4801) and to the Ministry of Economy and Competitiveness (CTQ 2014-52939R) for financial support. S. Benítez-Martínez is also grateful to Junta de Andalucía for the award of a Research Training Fellowship.

One-pot hydrothermal synthesis of graphene quantum dots surface-pasivated by polyethylene glycol and their photoelectric conversion under near-infrared light
Graphene quantum dots as sensor for phenol in olive oil

Multifunctional graphene quantum dots for simultaneous targeted cellular imaging and drug delivery

Rapid analysis of gold nanoparticles in liver and river water samples
Graphene quantum dots sensor for the determination of graphene oxide in environmental water samples
Blue luminescent graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide prepared by tuning the carbonization degree of citric acid
